name: Pericarditis
identifyBy: Diffuse saddle-back (concave-up) ST elevation in multiple leads (I, II, III, aVF, V2–V6)
PR segment depression in multiple leads (reciprocal PR elevation in aVR)
ST elevation is concave/saddle-back, NOT convex (unlike STEMI)
Reciprocal ST depression ONLY in aVR and V1 (vs STEMI with territory-specific reciprocal changes)
Pleuritic chest pain - worsens lying flat, improves leaning forward

measurements.prInterval: Normal to slightly prolonged (PR depression is segment, not interval)

management: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400–800mg TDS or aspirin 500–1000mg TDS): first-line
Colchicine 0.5mg BD for 3 months: reduces recurrence rate significantly
Corticosteroids only if NSAID/colchicine failure (risk of recurrence/chronicity)
Investigate cause: idiopathic/viral most common; also bacterial, TB, autoimmune, uraemia, malignancy
Cardiac MRI if effusion or haemodynamic compromise suspected
Large effusion with haemodynamic compromise: pericardiocentesis urgently
pearls: PR depression + diffuse saddle-back ST elevation = pericarditis until proven otherwise
Spodick's sign: downward slope of TP segment (highly specific when present)
STEMI mimic: pericarditis ST is diffuse and concave; STEMI is territorial and convex
aVR is the key lead: ST depression + PR elevation in aVR is characteristic of pericarditis
Athlete's heart (early repolarisation) can look similar - PR segment is the key differentiator (normal PR in ER)
differentials: STEMI (convex ST, territorial distribution, reciprocal changes, wall motion abnormality)
Early repolarisation (no PR depression, young/athletic patient, no pain progression)
Myocarditis (similar ECG, more severe, troponin rise, wall motion abnormality)
Takotsubo/stress cardiomyopathy (diffuse ST, different wall motion pattern)
